refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) remains at or below the customer-defined E_target throughout the evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a high-level KPI requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional requirements. The refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level outcome dependent on multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, idle management, load optimization). The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation context (nominal conditions, evaluation period, worksite scenarios) to enable testability and allocation.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry collection of energy consumption (kWh or fuel equivalent) and work output (tons moved) across representative worksite missions; post-evaluation statistical analysis of energy_per_ton against E_target with confidence intervals; traceability to sub-system energy budgets.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during motion and load transport.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize parasitic energy losses in actuation and control.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize communication and processing overhead energy consumption.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize idle and standby energy consumption.
FR-EE-005: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility to operators for behavioral optimization.
FR-EE-006: The safety services subsystem shall not introduce energy penalties that exceed acceptable thresholds relative to baseline safety-critical operations.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement energy budgeting and closed-loop control across all subsystems with continuous telemetry feedback and operator dashboards; allocate energy targets to propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation, and idle management; enforce via real-time monitoring and advisory alerts.

DP-B: Optimize hardware efficiency (motor selection, hydraulic pump efficiency, communication protocol bandwidth) and implement software-level power management (dynamic frequency scaling, selective sensor sampling, idle timeouts); measure aggregate energy_per_ton post-mission.

DP-C: Decompose energy efficiency into subsystem-level KPIs (propulsion efficiency %, hydraulic loss %, teleoperation overhead %, idle power draw); allocate targets; implement independent subsystem optimization; aggregate via system-level energy accounting.

DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining hardware optimization (DP-B), subsystem energy budgeting (DP-C), and real-time operator feedback (DP-A) with adaptive control that adjusts operational parameters based on mission profile and energy state.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid energy efficiency architecture combining (1) hardware-level optimization (efficient motors, pumps, communication protocols), (2) subsystem energy budgeting with allocated targets for propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation, and idle management, (3) real-time energy telemetry collection and operator dashboards for behavioral feedback, and (4) adaptive control logic that adjusts operational parameters (speed profiles, sensor sampling rates, idle thresholds) based on mission profile and cumulative energy state to maintain energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

potential_sources: Propulsion efficiency and teleoperation overhead are coupled: higher teleoperation latency may require conservative speed profiles, reducing propulsion efficiency. Mitigation: allocate separate energy budgets and monitor coupling via telemetry.

Safety services (e.g., collision avoidance, emergency braking) may introduce energy penalties. Mitigation: FR-EE-006 explicitly bounds acceptable safety overhead; design safety logic to minimize energy impact.
Operator behavior and energy efficiency are coupled: aggressive acceleration/deceleration increases energy consumption. Mitigation: real-time feedback (DP-A component) enables operator learning.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite solution that addresses energy efficiency across multiple domains (hardware, software, control, human factors). Coupling is managed through explicit subsystem budgeting, telemetry feedback, and adaptive control. No fundamental design contradiction identified.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined E_target is technically achievable with current hardware and operational constraints. RISK: If E_target is unrealistic, system will fail acceptance. MITIGATION: Validate E_target against baseline energy models early in design.

ASSUMPTION: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, communication) can reliably measure energy consumption and work output with acceptable accuracy (±5%). RISK: Measurement error propagates to energy_per_ton calculation. MITIGATION: Calibrate sensors; implement redundant measurement paths.

ASSUMPTION: Operators will adopt energy-efficient behaviors when provided real-time feedback. RISK: Behavioral change may not occur; energy efficiency remains suboptimal. MITIGATION: Include operator training; design feedback UI for clarity and actionability.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive control logic can be tuned to balance energy efficiency with safety and productivity without introducing instability. RISK: Poorly tuned control may oscillate or degrade performance. MITIGATION: Extensive simulation and field testing before deployment.

ASSUMPTION: Safety services overhead can be bounded to acceptable levels (e.g., <5% of total energy budget). RISK: Safety logic may consume more energy than anticipated. MITIGATION: Design safety algorithms for energy efficiency; allocate explicit energy budget to safety.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Hardware optimization (fixed, design-time), (2) Subsystem energy budgeting (configuration, pre-mission), (3) Real-time telemetry and feedback (operational, continuous), (4) Adaptive control (operational, dynamic). This separation enables independent verification and reduces coupling. Additionally, define energy accounting methodology (what is included in 'energy_used'? fuel consumption? electrical draw? thermal losses?) to ensure consistent measurement across all subsystems.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the energy efficiency KPI, system-level energy accounting, adaptive control orchestration, and operator dashboards. It coordinates subsystem energy budgets and enforces energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through closed-loop monitoring and advisory/enforcement mechanisms.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution exports real-time energy consumption (kWh, fuel equivalent), work output (tons moved), subsystem efficiency metrics, and energy state to external monitoring systems and operator dashboards. Frequency: 1 Hz or mission-dependent sampling.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives mission profiles, work objectives, and operational constraints (e.g., time limits, safety zones) from Site Manager or planning system. Tasking includes energy budget guidance and priority (energy efficiency vs. productivity trade-offs).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution exchanges safety-critical alerts with Safety Services. Inbound: collision warnings, emergency stop signals. Outbound: energy-aware safety status, adaptive control state that may affect safety margins.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link for teleoperation command/feedback (to Teleoperation module) and for external telemetry export (to monitoring systems). Bandwidth and latency constraints affect teleoperation overhead energy consumption.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the energy efficiency KPI. It integrates all subsystems and owns system-level energy accounting, adaptive control orchestration, and operator feedback. Minimal diffusion: energy efficiency logic is concentrated here, with subsystem-level budgets delegated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Score reflects that NGHE Solution is the appropriate boundary for this FR.

Vehicle owns propulsion and hydraulic subsystems (FR-EE-001, FR-EE-002, FR-EE-004), which are primary energy consumers. However, Vehicle cannot own the system-level energy efficiency KPI or cross-subsystem adaptive control. Significant diffusion: energy efficiency requires coordination with Teleoperation (overhead) and Safety Services (safety penalties). Vehicle is a key contributor but not the owner.

Teleoperation owns communication and processing overhead (FR-EE-003) and contributes to operator feedback (FR-EE-005). However, it does not own propulsion, hydraulics, or safety integration. Significant diffusion: teleoperation energy efficiency is coupled to Vehicle performance and Safety Services constraints. Teleoperation is a contributor, not the owner.

Safety Services owns safety-critical operations and must ensure that energy efficiency measures do not compromise safety (FR-EE-006). However, Safety Services does not own the primary energy efficiency mechanisms. High diffusion: safety constraints are distributed across all subsystems. Safety Services is a constraint provider, not the owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-003 (Energy efficiency KPI) because: (1) Energy efficiency is a system-level outcome that depends on coordinated behavior across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) NGHE Solution owns system-level energy accounting, adaptive control orchestration, and operator dashboards; (3) NGHE Solution can enforce energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through closed-loop monitoring and advisory/enforcement mechanisms; (4) Subsystem-level energy budgets (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-006) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but NGHE Solution coordinates and validates their aggregate impact. This containment avoids diffusion of the KPI across multiple modules and enables clear accountability.
containment_risks: RISK: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' that owns too many concerns (energy accounting, adaptive control, operator feedback, subsystem coordination). MITIGATION: Clearly separate concerns within NGHE Solution (e.g., energy accounting module, adaptive control module, dashboard module) and define interfaces between them. Ensure NGHE Solution delegates subsystem-level optimization to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

RISK: Adaptive control logic in NGHE Solution may introduce coupling between subsystems that were previously independent. MITIGATION: Design adaptive control to operate on subsystem energy budgets and telemetry, not on internal subsystem state. Use well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) to minimize coupling.

RISK: Real-time energy telemetry and adaptive control may introduce latency or computational overhead that itself consumes energy. MITIGATION: Design telemetry collection and adaptive control algorithms for efficiency; use sampling and filtering to reduce overhead.

RISK: Operator dashboards and feedback mechanisms may not be effective in driving behavioral change, leaving energy efficiency suboptimal. MITIGATION: Include operator training and iterative UI refinement based on field feedback.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary for CR-NGHE-003. However, the internal structure of NGHE Solution should be refined to separate concerns: (1) Energy Accounting Module: owns telemetry collection, energy_per_ton calculation, and subsystem budget tracking. (2) Adaptive Control Module: owns control logic that adjusts operational parameters based on energy state and mission profile. (3) Operator Dashboard Module: owns real-time feedback UI and energy efficiency guidance. (4) System Orchestration Module: owns coordination between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. These internal modules should communicate via well-defined interfaces to avoid diffusion and enable independent testing. Additionally, define the energy accounting methodology (what is included in 'energy_used'?) and the telemetry schema (what metrics are collected, at what frequency?) to ensure consistency across subsystems.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (CR-NGHE-003) clearly states the energy efficiency objective (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and the evaluation context (nominal conditions, evaluation period, worksite scenarios). DP-D (hybrid energy efficiency architecture) directly addresses this objective through hardware optimization, subsystem budgeting, real-time telemetry, and adaptive control. The mapping is clear: DP-D provides the mechanisms to achieve the FR. No fundamental mismatch identified. The FR is testable (energy_per_ton can be measured) and the DP is implementable (all components are technically feasible).

diffusion_risks: DP-D requires coordination across Vehicle (propulsion, hydraulics), Teleoperation (communication overhead), and Safety Services (safety constraints). NGHE Solution must own the orchestration logic to avoid diffusion. MITIGATION: Define clear interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) between NGHE Solution and subsystems; ensure subsystem-level energy budgets are enforced locally, not globally.

Real-time adaptive control in NGHE Solution may introduce tight coupling between subsystems. MITIGATION: Design adaptive control to operate on subsystem energy budgets and telemetry, not on internal subsystem state; use feedback control loops with appropriate time constants to avoid instability.

Telemetry collection and energy accounting may be distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. NGHE Solution must aggregate and validate. MITIGATION: Define a standard telemetry schema and energy accounting methodology; require all subsystems to report via Telemetry interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary because it owns system-level energy accounting, adaptive control orchestration, and operator feedback. Subsystem-level energy optimization (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-006) is delegated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but NGHE Solution coordinates and validates their aggregate impact. This avoids diffusion of the KPI across multiple modules and enables clear accountability. Internal structure of NGHE Solution should separate concerns (energy accounting, adaptive control, operator dashboard, system orchestration) to minimize coupling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-003 is verifiable through continuous telemetry collection and post-evaluation statistical analysis. NGHE Solution owns the telemetry infrastructure (via Telemetry interface) and energy accounting logic, enabling direct measurement of energy_per_ton. Verification can be performed in field trials or controlled test environments. Acceptance criteria are clear: energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over the evaluation period.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CR-NGHE-003 is allocated to NGHE Solution, which owns system-level energy efficiency. Sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-006) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Allocation is clear and traceable. Each sub-FR has a defined owner and measurable success criteria.

recommended_revisions: Refine the energy accounting methodology: define what is included in 'energy_used' (fuel consumption? electrical draw? thermal losses?) and how it is measured across subsystems. This ensures consistent measurement and prevents gaming of the metric.

Define the telemetry schema: specify what metrics are collected (energy consumption, work output, subsystem efficiency, adaptive control state), at what frequency, and with what accuracy. This enables consistent data collection and analysis.
Establish energy budgets for each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) based on the overall E_target. This enables subsystem-level optimization and accountability.

Define the adaptive control algorithm: specify how NGHE Solution adjusts operational parameters (speed profiles, sensor sampling rates, idle thresholds) based on mission profile and energy state. This ensures control logic is predictable and testable.
Plan for operator training and UI refinement: energy efficiency depends on operator behavior. Include training and iterative UI design to maximize effectiveness of real-time feedback.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is a high-level KPI requirement that has been successfully refined into a clear FR, a comprehensive DP, and an appropriate IM assignment. The FR-DP-IM mapping is sound and passes all gate reviews. However, the requirement is not atomic: it depends on six sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-006) that must be further decomposed to enable detailed design and implementation. Specifically, FR-EE-001 (propulsion efficiency), FR-EE-002 (hydraulic efficiency), FR-EE-003 (teleoperation overhead), FR-EE-004 (idle management), FR-EE-005 (operator feedback), and FR-EE-006 (safety constraints) each require their own FR-DP-IM decomposition to define specific mechanisms, control algorithms, and module responsibilities. Proceeding to the next level of decomposition is necessary to translate the high-level energy efficiency strategy into implementable design decisions.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during motion and load transport.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize parasitic energy losses in actuation and control.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize communication and processing overhead energy consumption.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize idle and standby energy consumption.
FR-EE-005: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility to operators for behavioral optimization.
FR-EE-006: The safety services subsystem shall not introduce energy penalties that exceed acceptable thresholds relative to baseline safety-critical operations.

assumptions: Customer-defined E_target is technically achievable with current hardware and operational constraints.
Telemetry infrastructure can reliably measure energy consumption and work output with acceptable accuracy (±5%).
Operators will adopt energy-efficient behaviors when provided real-time feedback.
Adaptive control logic can be tuned to balance energy efficiency with safety and productivity without introducing instability.
Safety services overhead can be bounded to acceptable levels (e.g., <5% of total energy budget).
Energy efficiency is a system-level outcome that depends on coordinated behavior across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
NGHE Solution can own system-level energy accounting and adaptive control orchestration without becoming a 'god module'.
open_risks: E_target may be unrealistic; system may fail acceptance. MITIGATION: Validate E_target against baseline energy models early in design.
Measurement error in energy consumption or work output may propagate to energy_per_ton calculation. MITIGATION: Calibrate sensors; implement redundant measurement paths.
Operator behavioral change may not occur; energy efficiency remains suboptimal. MITIGATION: Include operator training; design feedback UI for clarity and actionability.
Adaptive control logic may introduce instability or degrade performance. MITIGATION: Extensive simulation and field testing before deployment.
Safety logic may consume more energy than anticipated. MITIGATION: Design safety algorithms for energy efficiency; allocate explicit energy budget to safety.
NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' with too many concerns. MITIGATION: Clearly separate concerns within NGHE Solution and define interfaces between them.
Real-time adaptive control may introduce tight coupling between subsystems. MITIGATION: Design adaptive control to operate on subsystem energy budgets and telemetry, not on internal subsystem state.

Telemetry collection and energy accounting may be distributed across subsystems, complicating aggregation and validation. MITIGATION: Define a standard telemetry schema and energy accounting methodology.
evidence_needed: Baseline energy models for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to validate E_target feasibility.
Sensor specifications and calibration procedures for energy consumption and work output measurement.
Operator training materials and UI mockups for real-time energy feedback.
Simulation models and test plans for adaptive control logic validation.
Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) to quantify safety services energy overhead.
Telemetry schema and energy accounting methodology documentation.
Subsystem energy budgets (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) derived from overall E_target.
Field trial plan and acceptance criteria for energy_per_ton measurement.
Operator feedback and behavioral change metrics from pilot deployments.
